name_arabic: فاطمة المدعوة ستيتة
name_english: Fatima, known as Sutayta (also called Najiya)
father: Judge Kamal al-Din Mahmud ibn Shirayin al-Hanafi
description_en: Fatima (called Sutayta) was a Cairo-born woman of extraordinary literary talent. She was described as 'brilliant in poetry composition, with excellent understanding and strength of spirit, unmatched in her qualities.' She married twice — first al-Nasiri Muhammad ibn al-Tunbugha, then Ala' Ali ibn Muhammad ibn Baybars (grandson of Sultan Barquq's sister). She performed hajj in 884 AH. Al-Sakhawi devoted 2,029 words to her — the longest women's entry in the entire dictionary.
notable: Longest women's entry in the entire Daw al-Lami'. Praised for intellectual brilliance and poetic composition.

name_arabic: أم هانئ بنت علي بن عبد الرحمن
name_english: Umm Hani bint Ali ibn Abd al-Rahman
father: Nur al-Din Abu al-Hasan Ali ibn al-Qadi Taqi al-Din
scholarly_activities: heard extensive hadith in Makkah at age 7
heard hadith in Cairo from multiple scholars
description_en: Umm Hani (also called Maryam) was born in Cairo in 778 AH to a distinguished scholarly family — granddaughter of Judge Fakhr al-Din al-Qayati. Her maternal grandfather took charge of her education, arranging for her to hear hadith in Makkah at age 7 from al-Nashawri and multiple other scholars, and in Cairo from another group. She became a significant hadith transmitter.
notable: Began formal hadith study at age 7 in Makkah. From a multi-generational scholarly family.

name_arabic: عائشة بنت علي بن محمد الكنانية
name_english: Aisha bint Ali ibn Muhammad al-Kinani
also_known_as: Called Sitt al-'Aysh (Lady of Life), also Umm Abdallah and Umm al-Fadl
teachers: her maternal grandfather Abu al-Haram (al-Fawa'id al-Ghaylaniyyat)
scholarly_activities: attended hadith sessions as a child
transmitted specific texts with documented chains
description_en: Aisha al-Kinani, called Sitt al-'Aysh, was a Hanbali woman from Cairo who began attending hadith sessions in childhood. She was presented before her maternal grandfather Abu al-Haram and heard five of eight sessions of al-Fawa'id al-Ghaylaniyyat, and studied with al-Izz ibn Jama'a and others. She transmitted texts with documented chains of authority.
notable: Hanbali woman — rare documentation of women in the Hanbali school. Multiple documented isnad chains.

name_arabic: رقية بنت الشرف محمد بن أبي الحسن
name_english: Ruqayya bint al-Sharaf Muhammad ibn Abi al-Hasan
father: al-Sharaf Muhammad, known as Ibn al-Qari'
family: From a hadith family. Her uncle Abu al-Faraj Abd al-Rahman was the musnad (highest authority) of Cairo. Wife of al-Qutb Abd al-Karim al-Halabi.
scholarly_activities: listed in her teacher's mu'jam (scholarly dictionary)
description_en: Ruqayya came from a distinguished hadith family of Damascus origin settled in Cairo. Her uncle was the musnad (highest hadith authority) of Cairo. She was mentioned in her teacher's mu'jam. Scholars sought her out and read to her, and her fame spread among hadith students. She granted ijaza to students — an act of scholarly certification.
notable: From a family where hadith scholarship was hereditary. Scholars came to HER to read — she was the authority, not the student.

name_arabic: هاجر بنت محمد بن محمد
name_english: Hajar bint Muhammad ibn Muhammad
also_known_as: Also called Aziza. Kunya: Umm al-Fadl
father: al-Muhaddith al-Sharaf Abu al-Fadl al-Qudsi al-Misri al-Shafi'i
scholarly_activities: became the highest isnad authority of her era
scholars crowded to study with her
description_en: Hajar (also called Aziza) was named after an older sister who died before her birth. Her father, a muhaddith, invested heavily in her education — presenting her and having her hear 'a great deal' of hadith collections, scholarly dictionaries, forty-hadith collections, and full books. By the end of her life, she became 'the highest isnad authority of her era' (asnad ahl asriha) and 'scholars crowded around her.'
notable: Described as 'the highest isnad authority of her era' — the most connected living link to the Prophet's words. Scholars crowded to study with her.

name_arabic: عائشة بنت محمد بن عبد الهادي المقدسية
name_english: Aisha bint Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi al-Maqdisiyya
also_known_as: Musnada al-Dunya (the world's highest hadith authority)
father: Muhammad ibn Abd al-Hadi ibn Qudama
family: Qurashi, Umari, Maqdisi — from the famous Ibn Qudama family
teachers: al-Sharaf Abdallah ibn al-Hasan (heard Sahih Muslim)
Abd al-Qadir ibn al-Muluk (heard Sirat Ibn Hisham)
description_en: Aisha al-Maqdisiyya was given the title 'Musnada al-Dunya' — literally 'the world's highest hadith authority.' From the famous Ibn Qudama scholarly dynasty of Damascus, she heard Sahih al-Bukhari, Sahih Muslim, and Sirat Ibn Hisham from named teachers with documented chains. She received ijaza from scholars across the region including Sitt al-Fuqaha ('Lady of the Jurists'), another woman scholar.
notable: MUSNADA AL-DUNYA — 'the world's highest hadith authority.' A woman held the highest certification in hadith transmission in the entire Islamic world. She also received ijaza from another woman (Sitt al-Fuqaha).

name_arabic: زينب بنت الكمال محمد بن الناصر
name_english: Zaynab bint al-Kamal Muhammad ibn al-Nasir
family: Mother of al-Najm Yahya and Zubayda. Aunt of the Army Comptroller.
husband: Sultan al-Zahir Inal (married during his emirate, ~825 AH)
description_en: Zaynab married the future Sultan Inal during his time as an emir, around 825 AH. Remarkably, he never married another woman nor took a concubine — all his children were from her. Al-Sakhawi notes this was 'unique among all the kings.' She had extraordinary political influence: 'the Sultan was extremely obedient to her commands, to the point where he had no choice with her.' She performed hajj during her time of power, which was described as an extraordinary event.
notable: Wife of Sultan Inal who exercised real political power. The Sultan 'had no choice' against her commands — and he never married another. 'Unique among all the kings.'

name_arabic: عائشة بنت إبراهيم بن خليل الزبيدية
name_english: Aisha bint Ibrahim ibn Khalil al-Zubaydi
scholarly_activities: heard extensive hadith from companions of Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari in Damascus, Cairo, and Baalbek
description_en: Aisha al-Zubaydi was from a scholarly family — her brother was al-Jamal Abdallah, a hadith hafiz. Born in Damascus, she heard extensive hadith from multiple scholars across three cities: Damascus, Cairo, and Baalbek. Her teachers were companions of the great Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari. She represents the geographic mobility of female scholarship — travelling for knowledge across the Islamicate world.
notable: Studied across THREE cities (Damascus, Cairo, Baalbek). Sister of a hadith hafiz. Shows women's geographic mobility for scholarship.

name_arabic: خديجة بنت أمير حاج بن البيسري
name_english: Khadija bint Amir Hajj ibn al-Baysari
family: Descended from Badr al-Din, a famous commander. Granddaughter of a candidate for the sultanate.
description_en: Khadija came from a powerful military family — her ancestor was a famous commander who was even considered for the sultanate. She married four times: her first husband was killed in Damascus, the second died, the third was the superintendent of Jeddah (she performed hajj with him and stayed in Makkah), and the fourth also died while they were together. Despite the tragedies, she maintained her status and performed the pilgrimage.
notable: From a family of military commanders considered for the sultanate. Married four times — each husband died. Shows elite women's resilience and continued social standing.

name_arabic: زينب بنت الكمال محمد بن الناصر بن البارزي
name_english: Zaynab bint al-Kamal Muhammad ibn al-Nasir ibn al-Barizi
family: Mother of al-Najm Yahya and Zubayda. Aunt of the Army Comptroller (Nazir al-Jaysh). Her father married two sisters consecutively — she and her half-sister Fatima are both daughters and cousins.
description_en: Zaynab was from the prominent al-Barizi family of scholars and administrators. Her father al-Kamal married two sisters consecutively, making Zaynab and her half-sister Fatima both daughters and maternal cousins — al-Sakhawi notes this unusual genealogical detail. She died on a Friday before the prayer in Muharram 875 AH, having gone the previous day to visit her sister who was the wife of the hajj commander Yashbak al-Jamali.
notable: From the al-Barizi administrative dynasty. Illustrates the interconnected elite families of the Mamluk period.

name_arabic: فاطمة المدعوة ستيتة ابنة قاناباي العمري
name_english: Fatima bint Qanabay al-'Umari al-Nasiri
family: Father was strangled when she was young. Raised by her mother Shukrbay, a Circassian formerly enslaved woman. Married twice — Taghri Birdi al-Mu'adhdhi then Jarbash Fashiq.
description_en: Fatima was orphaned young when her father was strangled. Raised by her Circassian mother. She married military commanders and her daughter Zaynab married Sultan al-Zahir Jaqmaq. Shows elite Mamluk women's political networks.
notable: Daughter's marriage to the Sultan. Illustrates women's political influence through marriage networks.

name_arabic: مغل بنت محمد بن عثمان خوند الكبرى
name_english: Mughal bint Muhammad ibn Uthman, 'al-Khawand al-Kubra' (the Great Lady)
family: From the al-Barizi family. Sister of al-Kamal Muhammad. Born during her family's flight from Timur's invasion.
description_en: Mughal was described as 'wonderfully beautiful.' Born during her family's flight from Timur's invasion. Her first husband died before consummation, then Sultan al-Mu'ayyad ordered her marriage to al-'Ilm Dawud against her father's wishes. Shows how state power intersected with women's lives.
notable: Born during flight from Timur. Forced marriage by Sultan's order. Named 'the Great Lady.'

name_arabic: نشوان (وتسمى سودة) بنت عبد الله الكنانية العسقلانية
name_english: Nashwan (also called Sawda) bint Abdallah al-Kinani al-'Asqalani
teachers: Ibrahim ibn Abi Bakr ibn al-Sallar (transmitted from al-Dimyati)
description_en: Nashwan received ijaza in an application dated Dhu al-Hijja 793 AH from multiple scholars, including one who transmitted by ijaza from al-Dimyati — connecting her to a chain going back to the great hadith master.
notable: Connected to al-Dimyati's hadith chain through documented ijaza.

name_arabic: أم هانئ بنت محمد بن محمد بن فهد الهاشمية المكية
name_english: Umm Hani bint Muhammad ibn Fahd al-Hashimiyya al-Makkiyya
also_known_as: Also called Zaynab. 'Sayyidat Bani Hashim' (Lady of the Hashimites)
family: Sister of al-Najm ibn Fahd and siblings. Hashimite lineage.
description_en: Umm Hani, called 'Lady of the Hashimites,' was from the prominent Ibn Fahd scholarly family of Makkah. From age 2, she was presented at hadith sessions. She heard from al-Shams ibn al-Jazari (the great Quran reciter) and multiple other scholars. She studied the Mashyakha of al-Fakhr, hadith portions, and al-Burda.
notable: Hashimite lineage. Began attending hadith sessions at age 2. Studied under Ibn al-Jazari, the master of Quranic recitation.

name_arabic: مريم بنت أحمد بن محمد الأذرعية المصرية
name_english: Maryam bint Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Adhra'i al-Misri
family_origin: Originally from Adhra'at (Daraa, Syria), then Aleppo, then Damascus, then Cairo
description_en: Maryam came from a family of judges — her grandfather held the judiciary in Damascus then Cairo. Her father taught at the Mosque of al-Hakim. She lived so long that she 'became unique' (infaradat) — meaning she was the last surviving link in certain hadith chains, making her indispensable for transmission.
notable: Became the sole surviving link in hadith chains through longevity. Family of judges from Syria to Cairo — shows scholarly migration patterns.

name_arabic: زاده خوندشاه بنت أرض بك بن محمد الرومية
name_english: Zadeh Khawandshah bint Ard Bek ibn Muhammad, the Ottoman princess
family: Granddaughter of Bayezid I ibn Uthman (the Ottoman Sultan). Sister of Suleiman.
description_en: An Ottoman princess — granddaughter of Sultan Bayezid I. She and her brother Suleiman came to Cairo as refugees, where Sultan al-Ashraf Barsbay received them and housed them in the royal quarters of the Citadel. Later, some Ottomans plotted to smuggle them back — a ship arrived at Rosetta disguised as merchants, loaded with provisions and fighters. Shows the Mamluk-Ottoman political connections.
notable: Ottoman princess, granddaughter of Sultan Bayezid I, living as a refugee in Mamluk Cairo. Dramatic escape attempt documented.

name_arabic: أنس بنت عبد الكريم بن أحمد اللخمي النستراوي
name_english: Anas bint Abd al-Karim ibn Ahmad al-Lakhmi al-Nistrawi
family: Wife of al-Sakhawi's own teacher (shaykh). Her mother was Sarah bint Nasir al-Din, granddaughter of Mankutimur, the Sultan's viceroy.
description_en: Anas was the wife of al-Sakhawi's own shaykh (teacher), making this entry deeply personal. She came from a powerful family — her maternal grandmother was the granddaughter of Mankutimur, the Sultan's viceroy, who owned a famous madrasa. Al-Sakhawi knew her personally.
notable: Wife of al-Sakhawi's own teacher. Personal connection to the author. Descended from the Sultan's viceroy.

name_arabic: أم هانئ بنت علي بن محمد بن ظهيرة القرشية المكية
name_english: Umm Hani bint Ali ibn Zahira al-Qurashiyya al-Makkiyya
family: Sister of the Qadi (judge) and scholar of the Hijaz, al-Burhani. Eldest daughter.
description_en: Umm Hani was the eldest daughter and sister of the chief judge and leading scholar of the Hijaz region. She received ijaza from al-Taqi al-Fasi and others. She married her cousin Abu al-Fadl Muhammad. From the prominent Ibn Zahira Qurashi family of Makkah — a scholarly dynasty.
notable: Sister of the chief judge of the Hijaz. From the Ibn Zahira scholarly dynasty.

name_arabic: زينب بنت أحمد بن محمد الدمشقية الشوبكية المكية
name_english: Zaynab bint Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Dimashqiyya al-Makkiyya
description_en: Zaynab was born in Makkah. Her father died when she was an infant (800 AH). At age 5 (804 AH), she was presented at hadith sessions where she heard Sunan Ibn Majah (with some missing parts), al-Aslaf al-Nabawiyya, Juz' Abi Ya'la al-Khalili, and other texts. She transmitted hadith with documented chains.
notable: Began formal hadith study at age 5, one year after her father's death. Orphaned scholar who continued in knowledge.

name_arabic: باي خاتون بنت علي بن محمد الأنصارية الدمشقية
name_english: Bay Khatun bint Ali ibn Muhammad al-Ansariyya al-Dimashqiyya
description_en: Bay Khatun was from a distinguished Ansari (Medinan companion) lineage. Born in Damascus, she moved to Cairo. She heard hadith from al-Taqi Abu Bakr al-Mizzi and al-Kamal. Her genealogy traces back through 14 generations to the Khazraj tribe of Medina — one of the two Ansari tribes who supported the Prophet.
notable: 14-generation genealogy traced to the Khazraj. Ansari lineage maintained across centuries from Medina to Damascus to Cairo.

name_arabic: سارة بنت عمر بن عبد العزيز بن جماعة
name_english: Sarah bint Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz ibn Jama'a
family: From the famous Ibn Jama'a scholarly dynasty of Hama then Cairo. Sister of Abdallah.
description_en: Sarah was from the famous Ibn Jama'a family — one of the most prominent judicial and scholarly dynasties spanning Hama and Cairo. Born around 760 AH. She received ijaza from companions of Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari and others, connecting her chains through the most prestigious hadith networks.
notable: From the Ibn Jama'a dynasty — one of the most prestigious scholarly families in Islamic history.

name_arabic: فاطمة بنت خليل بن أحمد الكنانية المقدسية
name_english: Fatima bint Khalil ibn Ahmad al-Kinani al-Maqdisi al-'Asqalani
family: Wife of al-Shihab Ghazi al-Hanbali. Niece of the judge Nasir al-Din.
description_en: Fatima al-Kinani received ijaza in 754 AH and after from a remarkable list of authorities including al-Salah al-'Ala'i (the great Shafi'i jurist and hadith critic), al-Taqi al-Subki (the famous theologian), and al-'Izz ibn Jama'a. The quality of her teachers represents the highest tier of 14th-century scholarship.
notable: Studied under al-Taqi al-Subki and al-Salah al-'Ala'i — two of the most celebrated scholars of the 14th century.

name_arabic: زينب بنت عبد الرحيم بن الحسين العراقي
name_english: Zaynab bint Abd al-Rahim al-'Iraqi
also_known_as: Umm Muhammad. Daughter of al-Zayn al-'Iraqi.
father: al-Zayn al-'Iraqi — one of the greatest hadith scholars of the era
texts_transmitted: Musnad Ahmad (heard from her father and al-Haythami)
description_en: Zaynab was the daughter of al-Zayn al-'Iraqi, one of the most celebrated hadith scholars of the era (author of Alfiyyat al-'Iraqi). Her brother confirmed she began attending sessions before age 5. She heard from her father, al-Haythami, and others. She transmitted parts of Musnad Ahmad ibn Hanbal — one of the most important hadith collections.
notable: Daughter of al-Zayn al-'Iraqi (one of the greatest hadith masters). Transmitted Musnad Ahmad. Scholarly lineage through both blood and study.

name_arabic: آمنة بنت محمد بن علي العدوي القاهري المالكي
name_english: Amina bint Muhammad ibn Ali al-'Adawi al-Qahiri al-Maliki
family: AL-SAKHAWI'S OWN MOTHER. He writes: 'my mother.'
description_en: This is AL-SAKHAWI'S OWN MOTHER. He writes tenderly: she grew up under her parents' care, married a jurist who died, then married al-Sakhawi's father who had several children with her including 'its writer' (the author himself). She performed hajj with him multiple times and resided in both Makkah and Madinah. Al-Sakhawi includes his own mother in his biographical dictionary.
notable: AL-SAKHAWI'S OWN MOTHER included in the biographical dictionary. He writes about himself as 'its writer' (katibahu). The most personal entry in the entire work.

name_arabic: عائشة بنت إبراهيم بن خليل الزبيدية السنجارية
name_english: Aisha bint Ibrahim ibn Khalil al-Zubaydi al-Sinjariyya
also_known_as: Known as Ibn al-Shara'ihi. Umm Abdallah.
family: Sister of al-Jamal Abdallah, the hafiz. Originally from Sinjar, then Baalbek, then Damascus.
description_en: Already translated above — sister of a hadith hafiz, studied across Damascus, Cairo, and Baalbek with companions of Fakhr ibn al-Bukhari.
notable: Geographic mobility across three cities for hadith study.
